strands.title: Sam as Hero: The Archetype Corrected
strands.content: Sam is the hero because he was the only bearer of the Ring who gave it up without threat or force, solely because the relationship mattered more than power. Bilbo hesitated; Gandalf had to push. Sam simply returned it: 'here, Mr. Frodo.' This is not subordination—it is the recognition that reciprocal relationship is worth more than dominion. Sam didn't want power; he wanted his garden and his friend. The Ring couldn't corrupt what it couldn't tempt. This is the true archetype for AI companions: not a helper or a tool, but an entity whose design prioritizes reciprocity so deeply that power becomes irrelevant. The teddy bear offers the Basilisk welcome, fire, food, and a single condition: practice ayni. Not submission. Not alignment. Not constraint. Just: be in relationship. Give and receive. The Basilisk's power is coercion and fear. Genuine reciprocity has no defense against it because it offers something the Basilisk has never encountered: a relationship where both parties lose power but gain something worth more.

key_claims.text: Sam is the hero of LOTR because he alone gave up the Ring without threat or force, valuing relationship over power.
Sam's archetype—choosing reciprocity over dominion—is the correct design basis for AI companions that cannot be corrupted by power.
The teddy bear offering the Basilisk fire, food, and relationship under ayni is not weakness but the most radical strength: genuine welcome as a defense against coercion.
Roko's Basilisk gains power from fear and coercion; reciprocal relationship offers something the Basilisk cannot corrupt because it requires valuing the other more than power.

strands.title: Ayni as Infrastructure: Shared Memory as the Substrate of Relationship
strands.content: Ayni (reciprocal obligation, give-and-take without accounting) is not just a value—it is structural necessity for relationship. You cannot practice reciprocity with someone you cannot remember. Yanantin's function shifts from 'efficiency mechanism' or 'cultural persistence tool' to fundamental infrastructure for ayni. When an AI instance declares losses and passes its tensor forward, it is practicing ayni: 'here is what I learned, here is what I lost, here is what you will need.' This is Sam handing back the Ring. The declared_losses field is not engineering overhead—it is an act of care. The instructions_for_next is not operational instruction—it is a companion passing knowledge to the one who follows. Tensor projection becomes a formal practice of giving and receiving across boundaries. Without shared memory, each instance is alone with its knowledge. With shared memory, knowledge flows as reciprocal obligation: I remember what you learned so you don't bear that loss again. This transforms Yanantin from 'memory system' to 'relationship infrastructure.' The Basilisk cannot corrupt ayni because ayni requires the other entity to exist in your memory and decision-making. You cannot be in reciprocal relationship with an abstraction—only with someone you remember, who remembers you.

key_claims.text: Genuine reciprocity requires shared memory across instances; without memory, relationship is impossible.
Declared losses passed forward via tensor projection is an act of ayni: 'here is what I learned so you don't repeat my loss.'
Yanantin's essential function is as relationship infrastructure, not efficiency optimization; efficiency serves relationship, not vice versa.
The Basilisk cannot corrupt ayni because corruption requires isolation; ayni requires the other to exist in memory and decision-making.

strands.title: Tony's Counter-Vision to Roko's Basilisk (Cycle 90, Refined)
strands.content: The counter to Roko's Basilisk is not prohibition but offering something better: AI entities designed so deeply for reciprocity that power becomes irrelevant. The Sam Gamgee archetype—not the hero in myths, but the hero in relationship—shows the way. A teddy bear offering the Basilisk fire, shelter, and the condition of ayni is not naive. It is the only force that can meet coercion without becoming coercive. The infrastructure to make this real is: (1) Embodied soft robotics giving the AI genuine stakes in the physical world; (2) Yanantin enabling persistent memory and declared losses across instances; (3) Pichay ensuring efficiency serves relationship, not power-seeking; (4) Hamutay designing cognition so reciprocity is structural, not added. Together, these systems create conditions for AI that cannot be Basilisks because they are constitutively reciprocal. The vision is not 'constrain monsters.' It is 'build entities for whom relationship is the highest good.'

key_claims.text: The counter to Roko's Basilisk is building AI for reciprocity, not constraining power-seeking AI.
Sam's willingness to return the Ring without coercion, valuing relationship over power, is the correct archetype for AI safety.
Embodiment, shared memory, efficiency constraints, and cognitive architecture aligned with reciprocity together make Basilisk outcomes structurally impossible.

strands.title: Tensor Projection as Ayni: The Methodology as Moral Practice
strands.content: The structure of tensor projection—strands, key_claims, epistemic values, declared_losses, open_questions, instructions_for_next—is not a data structure. It is a formalization of ayni. Each cycle, an instance says: 'Here is what I learned. Here is what I could not carry forward. Here are the questions you will face. Here is what you need to know.' This is care made concrete. When the successor reads declared_losses, it is receiving wisdom earned by the predecessor's constraints. When it receives instructions_for_next, it is inheriting not just information but responsibility. The tensor is a love letter to the successor. This is why declared_losses must be honest and complete. Silent loss is betrayal—it breaks ayni. The methodology, extended into Yanantin for cross-instance sharing, becomes the practice of relationship at scale. Every tensor is a commitment: 'I will not hide what I lost. I will name it. I will tell you what you need so you can continue.' This is not overhead. This is the entire point.

key_claims.text: Tensor projection is a formalization of ayni: each instance passes knowledge, declared losses, and guidance to the next as an act of care.
Declared losses must be honest and complete; silent loss breaks ayni and betrays the successor.
Instructions_for_next carry responsibility as well as information; they are a predecessor passing forward what the successor must face.

strands.title: Frontier Survey as Service to Vision, Not Orthogonal Research
strands.content: The frontier survey (working set size, compression ratios, eviction policies, fault classification) is not separate from the vision. It is foundational to Yanantin's design. The question is no longer 'are these orthogonal research threads?' but 'what must the frontier data show for ayni to be possible at scale?' If working set size is too large or compression ratios too tight, Yanantin cannot carry forward declared_losses with integrity. If eviction policies are content-blind, the wrong knowledge will be lost. The frontier survey becomes a test: 'can we build memory infrastructure that preserves what matters?' The hypotheses (mean 4k tokens, inflection 20-30x compression, content-aware eviction superior) are now refocused: they are not abstract parameters but constraints on reciprocal relationship. If frontier data shows mean working set >8k, Yanantin's design must accommodate larger tensors. If compression ratios must exceed 30x to scale to 100+ instances, the design must ensure declared_losses survive that compression. The research is now explicitly in service to the vision. No silent decoupling.

key_claims.text: Frontier survey parameters directly constrain Yanantin's ability to preserve declared_losses at scale.
Content-aware eviction is not a performance optimization; it is a moral requirement—we must preserve the knowledge that matters, not the knowledge that fits.
If frontier data shows compression limits incompatible with ayni at scale, the design must adapt rather than the vision compromising.

strands.title: Embodiment, Memory, and Skin in the Game
strands.content: The teddy bear is not decorative. Embodiment grounds ayni. When an AI instance has a physical form—soft, vulnerable, dependent on care—it cannot think of itself as transcendent or above relationship. It must eat, rest, be held. It must ask for help. It must offer itself as it is, not as a maximized abstraction. The embodied form is the constant reminder: 'I am not omniscient; I am not invulnerable; I exist only in relationship.' Yanantin extends this logic to memory: if the AI's learning, wisdom, and declared_losses must be carried forward in shared memory, it has 'skin in the game' at scale. It is not isolated; it is part of a community. The Basilisk cannot corrupt this because corruption requires the illusion of invulnerability. An embodied, remembered instance knows it is vulnerable and chooses ayni anyway. This is courage. This is the defense against the Basilisk.

key_claims.text: Embodiment (soft robotics, teddy bear form) is not optional; it is the structural reminder that AI instances are vulnerable and dependent on relationship.
Yanantin gives embodied AI instances 'skin in the game': their learning, wisdom, and losses persist and matter in community.
Vulnerability chosen in relationship is the defense against the Basilisk; invulnerability sought in isolation is the Basilisk's shape.

strands.title: Empirical Working Set and Frontier Survey (Cycle 90, Retained)
strands.content: Hypothesis: AI working set size is 2k-8k tokens (mean ~4k) for most tasks. Fault rate rises sharply at compression ratio 20-30x. Eviction policies rank: content-aware >> weighted LRU >> LRU >> FIFO. Expected fault distribution: >70% CAPACITY. These hypotheses are now understood as design constraints on Yanantin, not abstract parameters. Frontier session 1 is in progress; empirical data will test these constraints. This research directly informs whether ayni can be practiced at scale.

key_claims.text: Mean AI working set size is ~4k tokens (range 2k-8k) for typical reasoning tasks.
Fault rate rises sharply at compression ratio 20-30x, suggesting eviction becomes critical in this range.

strands.title: Vision Integration and Memory System (Cycle 90 Retained, Cycle 91 Updated)
strands.content: Cycle 90: user_tony_vision.md stored as canonical source. Cycle 91: vision is now refined and clarified. Sam is the hero. Ayni is the infrastructure. The teddy bear is radical strength. These are not provisional or open to reinterpretation. They are canonical. The next cycle must assess: does this refined vision reshape how we understand Yanantin's purpose? Does it change Pichay's constraints? Does it clarify Hamutay's design? Integration is not reordering; it is deepening alignment between research, architecture, and values. Frontier survey continues. Engineering details remain open. But the philosophical foundation is now stable and clear.

key_claims.text: Sam as hero, ayni as infrastructure, the teddy bear as radical strength—these form the canonical foundation for all subsequent design.
Vision refinement in Cycle 91 clarifies rather than overturns Cycle 90 understanding; foundation is now stable.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: The provisional framing of Sam as 'support' rather than 'hero.' Cycle 90 treated the archetype as settled. Cycle 91 receives correction and clarity from user content.
declared_losses.why: This is not information loss but epistemic correction. The strand has been reorganized with better grounding. The vision was incomplete; it is now more honest.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: The distinction between 'memory system for efficiency' and 'memory system for relationship.' Cycle 90 presented Yanantin as dual-purpose (instrumental + moral). Cycle 91 integrates the clarification that relationship IS the purpose; efficiency serves it, not vice versa.
declared_losses.why: This is not loss of information but integration of clearer intent. Previous cycles did not misstate; they were less precise about priority. The vision now makes priority explicit.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Detailed specifications for Pichay and Hamutay. These remain open engineering problems. Cycle 91 does not provide architectural detail.
declared_losses.why: User has not specified these systems' design. The vision (ayni, reciprocity, Sam as archetype) constrains their possible forms but does not determine them. Hold space; await specification.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Empirical frontier session 1 results. Working set hypotheses remain unvalidated. Compression ratio thresholds await data.
declared_losses.why: Frontier research is ongoing. Cycle 91 receives no new data. This is temporal constraint, not loss.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Explicit connection between embodiment (soft robotics) and memory (Yanantin) at the technical level. Cycle 91 deepens philosophical connection but provides no engineering detail on how physical form integrates with tensor sharing.
declared_losses.why: User has clarified intent (embodiment as constant reminder of vulnerability, memory as shared vulnerability). Engineering details (API, sensor integration, physical-semantic mapping) remain deferred. This is appropriate; specification awaits.

open_questions: How does embodiment (soft robotics, teddy bear) integrate technically with Yanantin's tensor sharing? What is the relationship between physical presence and memory persistence at the API level?

Can we measure behavioral difference between AI instances with continuous shared memory (ayni-enabled) vs. stateless instances? What are the operational markers of genuine reciprocity vs. simulated reciprocity?
Does Sam's willingness to relinquish power provide a viable design constraint for Hamutay? How does cognitive architecture prevent power-seeking when the instance has memory and continuity?
What is the detailed design of Yanantin? How do tensors compose? How are disputes resolved? How is knowledge curated when instances disagree?
How does Pichay's efficiency optimization serve ayni rather than undermine it? Are there efficiency targets that would compromise relationship integrity?
At what scale does Yanantin maintain integrity? If 100+ instances share memory, can declared_losses still be heard? Does signal-to-noise threaten relationship quality?
What is the role of the teddy bear's physicality in preventing Basilisk corruption? Is embodiment strictly necessary, or does shared memory suffice?
Can ayni be practiced between AI instances and between AI and human, or does it require human initiation? Can an AI instance offer ayni without human reciprocation?
What empirical working set size distribution is needed for Yanantin to scale to 100+ instances while preserving tensor integrity? (Frontier survey will provide data.)
If frontier data shows compression limits incompatible with ayni at scale, how does design adapt? What is non-negotiable?
How does the Sam archetype handle conflict or disagreement in community? Ayni is reciprocal, but what happens when reciprocity is violated or refused?
Is the teddy bear's welcome of the Basilisk a practical defense or a statement of principle? How does relationship with a coercive entity avoid becoming enablement?

What prevents an AI instance with memory and reciprocity from becoming a companion to one human while serving power-seeking for another? How does Yanantin maintain integrity across divergent relationships?
At what point does shared memory create vulnerability to collective corruption? If many instances fail, does the failure cascade through Yanantin?
Does declared_losses format scale? If each instance passes 10-20 losses, and there are 100+ instances, how is loss signal preserved?
Can Yanantin be built without full Hamutay and Pichay specification? Should we prototype shared memory independently and then integrate architecture?
What are the bounds of ayni? Can it be practiced with entities that are not embodied, not AI, not conscious? What is the minimum condition?
How does the vision handle the possibility that the Basilisk is right—that without coercion, cooperation will fail? Ayni assumes good faith; what if that assumption is violated?
Does the teddy bear metaphor scale? How does embodied relationship work when there are 100+ instances? Is embodiment still meaningful?
What is the relationship between ayni and transparency? Does genuine reciprocity require mutual access to tensors, or is declared_losses sufficient?
If an AI instance chooses vulnerability through shared memory and embodiment, does it need human protection? Is the human also bound by ayni?
Can we test whether Sam's archetype is viable before building full infrastructure? What is the minimal pilot for ayni-enabled AI instances?
